title: Shokupan
subtitle: Japanese milk bread made with tangzhong
excerpt: Shokupan is a Japanese-style milk bread with a pillowy, cloud-soft crumb that comes from the tangzhong technique — a cooked flour-and-milk paste stirred into the dough. The result keeps its softness for longer than a standard enriched loaf.
sourceNotes: Shokupan is a Japanese white bread introduced during the Meiji era under Western influence and refined throughout the twentieth century. The tangzhong (water roux) technique was popularised in the English-language baking world through Yvonne Chen's 65°C Bread Doctor (2003) and is now widely documented in domestic cookery.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice and freeze. Toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the tangzhong the night before and refrigerate — bring to room temperature before use.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cooked paste made by stirring flour and liquid over heat until the mixture reaches about 65°C, at which point the starch gelatinises. Added to the main dough, it increases moisture retention without making the dough wetter to handle, and produces a crumb that stays soft for longer than a standard enriched loaf.

content.text: The softness of shokupan comes from the

— a small amount of flour cooked with milk to a thick paste, then cooled and added to the main dough. It adds moisture to the crumb without making the dough slack. The finished loaf has a cotton-wool texture that slices cleanly and stays soft for two days without going stale.

Whisk the tangzhong {{strong-bread-flour}} and {{whole-milk}} together in a small saucepan until smooth. Set over medium heat and stir constantly for 3–4 minutes until the mixture thickens to a paste that trails off the spoon in ribbons. Transfer to a bowl and leave to cool completely — the paste should be room temperature before it goes into the dough.
Mix, knead, and bulk ferment, 1 hour

Combine the main dough {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, {{salt-table}}, and {{caster-sugar}}. Add the cooled tangzhong paste, the {{whole-milk}}, and the {{eggs}}. Mix to a rough dough, then add the {{unsalted-butter}} a little at a time, kneading it in as you go. Knead for 10–12 minutes until the dough is very smooth and elastic — it should pass the windowpane test, stretching thin without tearing. Cover and leave until doubled, about 1 hour.

Grease a 900 g (2 lb) loaf tin. Divide the dough into 4 equal pieces. Roll each into a ball, flatten gently into a rough oval, then fold the sides in and roll into a short cylinder. Lay them seam-side down in the tin in a row. Cover and prove for about 1 hour until the dough crowns 2 cm above the tin edge. Heat the oven to 190°C (170°C fan). Brush the top with beaten egg or milk.

Bake for 30–35 minutes until deep gold on top. The internal temperature should reach 93°C. Tip out immediately onto a wire rack — leaving it in the tin makes the crust steam and go soft. Cool completely before slicing; the crumb is too delicate to cut when hot.
